result:
Lately, I’ve been thinking about the quiet, overlooked architecture of a Tuesday afternoon. 

There is something strangely beautiful about the "in-between" times of the human experience. We tend to focus so much on the crescendos—the weddings, the promotions, the big moves, the shattering heartbreaks—that we forget how much of life is actually composed of the hum. 

It’s the sound of a distant lawnmower three streets over. It’s the way the light hits a stack of unwashed dishes in the kitchen, turning soapy water into something that looks like liquid quartz. It’s the feeling of finally sitting down after a long day, when your brain finally stops running its frantic errands and just lets the silence settle around you.

I think there is a profound bravery in just existing in those gaps. When you aren’t "achieving" anything, when you aren’t "becoming" a better version of yourself, and when you aren’t actively solving a problem—you’re just there. You’re a witness to the passing of time. 

I wonder if we’ve lost the art of doing nothing. We are a species obsessed with optimization. We listen to podcasts at 1.5x speed, we curate our days to maximize productivity, and we treat our downtime as a resource to be managed rather than a meadow to wander through. 

But if you look at a tree, it isn’t "optimizing" its growth. It’s just drinking the rain and turning toward the sun. It grows in fits and starts, through droughts and winters, without a spreadsheet or a self-help book. It understands that existence is not a project to be completed; it’s a rhythm to be inhabited.

I find myself wishing that more people would give themselves permission to be unfinished. To be a little messy, a little aimless, and perfectly content with the fact that they haven’t "figured it all out" yet. Because the truth is, the view from the "in-between" is actually quite lovely, if you stop looking at your watch long enough to see it. 

What about you? What does your "in-between" look like lately?
